coredata.dc:title: Handheld detection strategy: Real-time on-site assay of Mn(VII) and GSH integrating ratiometric fluorescent carbon dots paper strip, smartphone, and 3D-printed accessory
coredata.dc:creator: Shi, Lihong
Feng, Jianyang
Li, Xianchan
Zhou, Wei
Zhang, Guomei
Zhang, Yan
Zhang, Caihong
Shuang, Shaomin
coredata.dc:description: Handheld detection platform integrating ratiometric fluorescent N,P co-doped carbon dots (N,P-CDs) paper strip, smartphone, and 3D-printed accessory corroborates its efficacy for visual quantitative detection of Mn(VII) and GSH via a continuous fluorescence color shift. N,P-CDs were synthesized by hydrothermal treatment of 1,8-diaminonaphthalene, phosphoric acid, and formamide. Inspiringly, with increasing Mn(VII) concentrations, blue emission of N,P-CDs decreases gradually at 445 nm and a ratio rising orange band emerges at 600 nm under 340 nm excitation, which concurrently exhibits continuous fluorescence color variation of blue, purplish blue, purplish pink, pink, to orange. Subsequent addition of GSH renders orange fluorescence of N,P-CDs@Mn(VII) gradually quench but blue peak slightly enhance, accompanied by the fluorescence color successively returning from orange to blue. The RGB values of the resulting images were precisely defined by a color recognition application within the smartphone, and the R/B values could be employed for Mn(VII) and GSH quantification with linear ranges of 0 − 40 μM and 0 − 28 μM, respectively, as well as limits of detection of 24.49 nM and 21.41 nM, respectively. It is solicitously anticipated that our research will provide novel insight for the development of effective sensing protocol and the enlargement of practical application prospect.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Handheld detection
Ratiometric fluorescence
Carbon dots
Mn(VII)
GSH

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Visual quantitative detection of Mn(VII) and GSH
Handheld quantification of Mn(VII) and GSH
Detection of Mn(VII) and GSH in tap water and lake water

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21974154 ), Recruitment Program of Global Experts (Youth).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Inner structure of 3D printed accessory. (B) Handheld detection process. (C) Smartphone analysis process.

(A) FL spectra of N,P-CDs in the presence of different concentrations of Mn(VII) (0 − 40 μM). (Inset: color changes of N,P-CDs solution under 340 nm excitation) (B) Relationship between ln (I600/I445 +0.3040) and Mn(VII) concentration. (C) Calculated CIE coordinate from FL spectra of N,P-CDs with different Mn(VII) concentrations. (D) FL spectra of N,P-CDs@Mn(VII) with adding various GSH concentrations (0 − 28 μM). (Inset: color changes of N,P-CDs@Mn(VII) with various concentrations of GSH under 340 nm excitation) (E) Relationship between FL intensity ratios (I600/I445) and GSH concentration. (F) Calculated CIE coordinate from FL spectra of N,P-CDs@Mn(VII) with various concentrations of GSH.
(A−F) High-resolution XPS of N1s and O1s for N,P-CDs, N,P-CDs@Mn(VII), and N,P-CDs@Mn(VII)@GSH. (G) FTIR spectrum of N,P-CDs, N,P-CDs@Mn(VII), and N,P-CDs@Mn(VII)@GSH.
Possible mechanism of N,P-CDs responding to Mn(VII) and GSH.

(A−B) Photos of N,P-CDs-based paper strips under UV light with different ions and different Mn(VII) concentrations. (C) Linear relationship between R/B value and Mn(VII) concentration. (D−E) Photos of N,P-CDs@Mn(VII)-based paper strips under UV light with different amino acids and different GSH concentrations. (F) Linear relationship between R/B value and GSH concentration.
Diagram of handheld detection of Mn(VII) and GSH.

head.ce:title: Handheld detection strategy: Real-time on-site assay of Mn(VII) and GSH integrating ratiometric fluorescent carbon dots paper strip, smartphone, and 3D-printed accessory

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, P. R China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Laboratory of Natural and Biomimetic Drugs and Department of Pharmaceutical Analysis, School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Peking University, Beijing 100191, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Laboratory of Natural and Biomimetic Drugs and Department of Pharmaceutical Analysis, School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Peking University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Laboratory of Natural and Biomimetic Drugs and Department of Pharmaceutical Analysis, School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Peking University, Beijing 100191, P. R China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Handheld detection platform integrating ratiometric fluorescent N,P co-doped carbon dots (N,P-CDs) paper strip, smartphone, and 3D-printed accessory corroborates its efficacy for visual quantitative detection of Mn(VII) and GSH via a continuous fluorescence color shift. N,P-CDs were synthesized by hydrothermal treatment of 1,8-diaminonaphthalene, phosphoric acid, and formamide. Inspiringly, with increasing Mn(VII) concentrations, blue emission of N,P-CDs decreases gradually at 445 nm and a ratio rising orange band emerges at 600 nm under 340 nm excitation, which concurrently exhibits continuous fluorescence color variation of blue, purplish blue, purplish pink, pink, to orange. Subsequent addition of GSH renders orange fluorescence of N,P-CDs@Mn(VII) gradually quench but blue peak slightly enhance, accompanied by the fluorescence color successively returning from orange to blue. The RGB values of the resulting images were precisely defined by a color recognition application within the smartphone, and the R/B values could be employed for Mn(VII) and GSH quantification with linear ranges of 0 − 40 μM and 0 − 28 μM, respectively, as well as limits of detection of 24.49 nM and 21.41 nM, respectively. It is solicitously anticipated that our research will provide novel insight for the development of effective sensing protocol and the enlargement of practical application prospect.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Quantitative measurement plays a pivotal part for real-time sensing and effective detection in a variety of domains including food/medicine quality management [1], environmental governance, clinical diagnosis [2], as well as social security assessment [3]. The majority of quantitative assays are conducted with the assistance of trained professionals and costly laboratory instruments, which are time-consuming, high-budget and not favorable for real-time on-site analyses [4]. Consequently, there is an urgent need to exploit a portable, straightforward, and speedy protocol for the efficacious identification of intended analytes, particularly in under-developed nations or resources-insufficient areas.

Handheld detection platform, integrating ratiometric fluorescent (FL) paper strip, smartphone, and 3D-printed optical accessory, might provide a universal strategy for real-time on-site visual quantitation [5]. On the current detection platform, the paper strip enables the visual detection [6], the smartphone makes the quantitative identification possible by calculating red, green, and blue (RGB) values through its built-in software [7], and 3D printing offers a portable device and defined slots for functional channels [8]. The merits of portability, intelligence, and rapidity endue the platform with further progress of quantitative analysis and wider range of real-time on-site sensing capabilities. Presently, the handheld detection platform has greatly motivated the interest of researchers. You et al. synthesized core-shell upconversion nanoparticles for handheld quantifying of brain natriuretic peptide and suppression of tumorigenicity 2 (a receptor glycoprotein) [9]. Guo et al. designed Ag nanoparticles/UiO-66-NH2 nanoprobe for handheld detection of glucose and cholesterol [10]. By covalently coupling fluorescein 5-isothiocyanate (FITC) with NH2-rich europium MOF (EuMOF), Wang et al. constructed handheld detection platform for identifying biogenic amines in food [11]. Chu et al. fabricated red-emitting CdTe quantum dots@silica nanoparticles@blue-emitting carbon dots (CDs) probe for handheld detection of pesticides [3]. Jiang et al. mixed CDs and gold nanoclusters to construct ratiometric emission probe for handheld determination of glutathione (GSH) [4]. Nevertheless, the FL probes for these handheld sensing platforms suffer from some drawbacks including high toxicity, sophisticated preparation procedures, and expensive starting materials. Therefore, it is extremely imperative for handheld detection to search for probes with low toxicity, ease of preparation, and low cost.

CDs, an emerging FL sensing material, have received great attention since they were accidentally discovered in 2004 [12]. With the advantages of facile preparation, tunable emission, eminent water solubility, superior stability, excellent biocompatibility, and low toxicity [13 − 18], CDs have blazed a new trail in FL sensing. The vast majority of CDs are generally constructed as single-emission intensity-changed sensors. In recent years, a few of CDs-based ratiometric FL sensors have been designed [19 − 24]. However, for handheld detection, single CDs-based ratiometric FL nanoprobe has not been reported.

Herein, utilizing the handheld detection platform, ratiometric emission N,P co-doped CDs (N,P-CDs) was first developed for visual quantitation of Mn(VII) and GSH ( Scheme 1). Mn(VII) is a trace element necessary for organisms, and it is of great significance to the metabolism, brain function, bone growth, and enzyme activity of the human body [25]. Nevertheless, excessive intake of Mn(VII) can lead to testiness, hallucination, depression, madness, neurological disorder, and even gene mutation [17,26]. Additionally, GSH, as the most abundant non-protein thiol in mammalian cells, can preserve the redox equilibrium, remove heavy metals, and protect cells from free radicals [27]. Abnormal content of GSH in human tissues or cells can give rise to multiple diseases, including atherosclerosis, liver damage, diabetes, stroke, epilepsy, and AIDS [23,28]. Hence, the detections of Mn(VII) and GSH are highly significant. Moreover, in real application, simultaneous detection of Mn(VII) and GSH can improve detection efficiency compared with single target analyte [29]. In present work, ratiometric emission N,P-CDs were fabricated for handheld detection of Mn(VII) and GSH by one-step hydrothermal treatment of 1,8-diaminonaphthalene, phosphoric acid, and formamide. As-prepared N,P-CDs exhibit intrinsic ratiometric FL variation (I600/I445) upon the introduction of Mn(VII), which is attributed to the oxidation of functional groups on the surface of N,P-CDs by Mn(VII). With subsequent addition of GSH, FL intensity ratio (I600/I445) of N,P-CDs is partially recovered due to the reduction of functional groups of N,P-CDs’ surface by GSH. Based on the sensing phenomena, ratiometric FL paper strips are favorably prepared, which manifest continuous color variation of blue, purplish blue, purplish pink, pink and orange with the addition of Mn(VII) and GSH. The handheld detection platform accomplishes visual quantitative detection of Mn(VII) and GSH with low limits of detection (LOD) of 24.49 nM and 21.41 nM, as well as linear ranges of 0 − 40 µM and 0 − 28 µM, respectively. The innovation points in our present work lie in the following aspects: (1) Handheld detection platform integrating FL paper strip, smartphone, and 3D-printed accessory was constructed. (2) Single ratiometric-emission N,P co-doped CDs was first developed for handheld detection of Mn(VII) and GSH. (3) High sensitivity is achieved due to well-resolved FL shift (blue and orange) of paper strip induced by Mn(VII) and GSH. The handheld detection protocol contributes to portable, rapid and intelligent detection, which opens up a new way for real-time on-site determination.

1,8-diaminonaphthalene (0.2 g), phosphoric acid (1 mL) and formamide (3 mL) were dispersed in 30 mL ultrapure water, and the mixture was further sealed in 50 mL hydrothermal reactor vessel which reacted at 200 °C for 5 h. Acquired crude products were naturally cooled, then centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 30 min in a high-speed centrifuge. Afterwards, obtained supernatant was purified by filtration through a 0.22 µm microporous membrane. Ultimately, prepared N,P-CDs were collected and preserved at 4 °C.

The detection of Mn(VII) was performed as follows. 50 μL N,P-CDs stock solution was introduced to 500 μL phosphate buffered saline (PBS) (pH 7.0), and then different concentrations of Mn(VII) standard solution were added. FL spectra were recorded under 340 nm excitation after incubating for 30 s
For sensing of GSH, different concentrations of GSH were dispersed to N,P-CDs@Mn(VII) solution (40 μM Mn(VII)). FL spectra were recorded under 340 nm excitation after incubating for 30 s

Handheld sensing platform is assembled from three parts: 3D printed accessory, paper-based sensor, and smartphone. 3D model of the accessory ( Fig. 1A) was constructed using 3D modeling software and then 3D printed accessory (Fig. S1) was obtained by It-600 printer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visual quantitative detection of Mn(VII) and GSH
ce:section.ce:para: Sensing of Mn(VII) and GSH was performed by the handheld detection platform (Fig. 1B). Smartphone with Color Picker application program (APP) was used to record FL color of filter paper strip and analyze RGB values (Fig. 1C).

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image displayed in Fig. S2A presents that N,P-CDs possess near-spherical morphology and outstanding dispersity. The lattice spacing of 0.21 nm is revealed by high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image (Fig. S2B), which is assigned to the (101) face of graphitic carbon. Fig. S2C illustrates that the size distribution of N,P-CDs falls in the scope of 3.3 − 7.3 nm and mean statistical particle size is calculated to be 5.1 ± 0.7 nm. Atomic force microscope (AFM) images (Fig. S2D−E) depict excellent dispersion of N,P-CDs. N,P-CDs’ height range is 3.0 − 4.6 nm with average height of 3.7 nm (Fig. S2F). Consistent results with TEM are obtained.

As shown in Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum of N,P-CDs (Fig. S3A), the broad band at 3207 cm−1 arises from the O–H/N–H stretching vibration. The peaks at 2824 cm−1 and 1439 cm−1 reveal the presence of C–H bond. The peak at 1698 cm−1 corresponds to the CO stretching vibration, and the peaks located at 1515 cm−1 and 819 cm−1 are attributed to the N − H bending vibration. The peak at 1043 cm−1 is due to the C−O stretching vibration. The peaks at 3028 cm−1 and 768 cm−1 correspond to existence of stretching vibration and bending vibration of =C−H. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) full spectrum of N,P-CDs (Fig. S3B) demonstrates four bands at 131.5, 282.1, 398.0, and 529.3 eV, pertaining to P2p, C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively. N,P-CDs’ C1s spectrum (Fig. S3C) is differentiated as three peaks, including CO/C−C (284.5 eV), C−N/C−O (285.4 eV), and CO (286.2 eV). High-resolution spectrum of N1s is separated into two peaks at 400.2 and 400.6 eV (Fig. S3D), attributing to presence of C−N and N − H. O1s spectrum (Fig. S3E) reveals two peaks of CO/P − O and C−O−C/C−OH/P − O, which are located at 530.7 and 532.0 eV, respectively. P2p spectrum (Fig. S3F) shows two peaks, implying the presence of two types of phosphorus bond: P − O (133.0 eV) and PO (134.0 eV).

As shown in UV–vis spectrum (Fig. S4), N,P-CDs demonstrate three characteristic absorption peaks. The peak at 208 nm illustrates the presence of π − π * transition of CC in the core of N,P-CDs. The peaks at 230 and 328 nm are owing to n − σ * transition of N − H/O−H and n − π * transition of CO of carboxyl groups on N,P-CDs surface, respectively. Solution of N,P-CDs is light yellow under sunlight and emits bright blue FL under UV light (inset in Fig. S4). And N,P-CDs’ emission shows excitation dependence when excitation wavelength increases from 300 to 380 nm.

To facilitate practical application, N,P-CDs’ stability was inspected. FL intensity of N,P-CDs is basically stable in wide pH value range from 3 to 12 (Fig. S5A). Various concentrations of NaCl (0 − 1 M) have no obvious effect on FL intensity of N,P-CDs as shown in Fig. S5B. In addition, the FL intensity of N, P-CDs remains stable even after continuous excitation for 20 min and storage for 20 d, which confirms the excellent stability of N, P-CDs (Fig. S5C−D). Such FL stability of N,P-CDs results from both preparation method and structural characteristics. On one hand, heteroatom doping has higher thermodynamic stability than undoped preparation methods [30]. On the other hand, as-prepared N,P-CDs’ surface is covered with carboxyl groups and negatively charged (Fig. S6). Because the same charges repel each other, N,P-CDs do not aggregate and show good stability [20]. N,P-CDs’ FL quantum yield (QY) measures up to 10.6 % with quinine sulfate as a reference. Above results exhibit superior optical stability of N,P-CDs, which endows N,P-CDs with practical application potential.

Selectivity of CDs is key to investigating their possible sensing applications. N,P-CDs’ selectivity was evaluated in the existence of typical metal ions and anions at pH 7.0 under 340 nm excitation, involving Al3+, Bi3+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cr3+, Cr6+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Na+, Ni2+, Mn(VII), Br−, Ac−, Cl−, CO3 2−, ClO−, ClO4 −, H2O2, EDTA, F−, I−, NO2 −, PO4 3−, PPi, S2−, SCN−, SO3 2−, and SO4 2−. As demonstrated in Fig. S7A−B, FL intensity ratio (I600/I445) increases significantly when Mn(VII) is introduced, compared to negligible effect of other analytes. Such an outcome demonstrates high specificity of N,P-CDs toward Mn(VII) over competitive ions, suggesting that N,P-CDs may be utilized for selective ratiometric identification of Mn(VII). What’s more, the presence of other interfering ions at concentrations up to 40 μM shows minimal effect on the monitoring of Mn(VII).

Sensing capability of N,P-CDs for Mn(VII) was explored in the range of 0 − 40 μM. N,P-CDs’ FL intensity at 445 nm drops gradually accompanying with the emergence of a ratio rising peak at 600 nm with increasing concentration of Mn(VII) ( Fig. 2 A). By virtue of distinct variations in FL intensity ratio of the two emission peaks, FL color of N,P-CDs solution changes progressively from blue, purplish blue, purplish pink, pink, to orange under 340 nm excitation (inset of Fig. 2A), implying that visual recognition of Mn(VII) is quite feasible. As demonstrated in Fig. 2B, a good linear correlation with R2 = 0.9977 is presented by plotting ln (I600/I445 +0.3040) and Mn(VII) concentration in the range of 5 − 40 μM. The LOD is determined as 7.16 nM on the basis of the signal/noise ratio of 3, which is much lower than the reported Mn(VII) probes (Table S1). FL color of N,P-CDs under different Mn(VII) concentrations is presented accurately by Commission Internationale de L′Eclairage (CIE) chromaticity diagram (Fig. 2C), which demonstrates a great consistency with FL color of N,P-CDs solution displayed in inset of Fig. 2A, and thus corroborates the feasibility of N,P-CDs for visual Mn(VII) recognition.

Selectivity of N,P-CDs@Mn(VII) was further appraised by exploring the effect of typical amino acids on the FL intensity ratio (I600/I445), including arginine (Arg), aspartic acid (Asn), glycine (Gly), cysteine (Cys), glutamic acid (Glu), isoleucine (Ile), valine (Val), leucine (Leu), lysine (Lys), methionine (Met), phenylalanine (Phe), proline (Pro), threonine (Thr), and tyrosine (Tyr). As shown in Fig. S7C, GSH renders I600/I445 of N,P-CDs@Mn(VII) significantly reduce by 95 %, Cys decreases I600/I445 by 31 %, and other amino acids exhibit negligible influence, which reveals high selectivity of N,P-CDs@Mn(VII) toward GSH over competitive amino acids. Furthermore, other high-concentration (up to 28 μM) competitive substances do not interfere with GSH detection.

We further explored the sensing ability of N,P-CDs@Mn(VII) for GSH in the scope of 0 − 28 μM. When GSH concentrations increase, FL intensity at 445 nm increases slightly while that at 600 nm decreases substantially (Fig. 2D), during which FL color of the solution returns from orange, pink, purplish pink, purplish blue, to blue (inset of Fig. 2D). FL intensity ratio (I600/I445) presents an excellent linear relationship with GSH concentrations in the range of 0 − 20 μM (Fig. 2E). Calculated LOD is 2.20 nM based on the signal/noise ratio of 3. The proposed GSH probe demonstrates superior sensitivity, compared with previously reported CDs-based GSH probes (Table S2). CIE diagram (Fig. 2 F) demonstrates an accurate FL color shift trend of N,P-CDs@Mn(VII) with various GSH concentrations, having a total agreement with FL color of N,P-CDs@Mn(VII) solution shown in inset of Fig. 2D.

XPS was implemented to exploit the ratiometric FL sensing mechanism. In contrast to N1s spectrum of N,P-CDs ( Fig. 3A), Mn(VII) makes N − O peak appear (Fig. 3B), confirming the oxidation of amino groups to nitro groups, while subsequent addition of GSH renders N − O peak disappear again (Fig. 3C), revealing the reduction of nitro groups to amino groups [31,32]. In O1s spectra (Fig. 3D−F), the similar phenomena appear. We also used FTIR to confirm sensing mechanism. As shown in FTIR spectra of N,P-CDs, N,P-CDs@Mn(VII) and N,P-CDs@Mn(VII)@GSH (Fig. 3G), as Mn(VII) is added the absorption peaks of stretching vibration (3207 cm−1) and bending vibrations (1515 cm−1 and 819 cm−1) of N − H are weakened obviously while the absorption peaks of asymmetric stretching vibration (1566 cm−1) and symmetric stretching vibration (1355 cm−1) of −NO2 are enhanced. Subsequent addition of GSH makes FTIR spectrum recover. FTIR results are coincident with XPS [33 − 35].

N,P-CDs’ surface is covered with amino, carboxyl, and hydroxyl groups. Amino groups on N,P-CDs’ surface may be oxidized to nitro groups by Mn(VII) in just a few minutes since Mn(VII) is a powerful oxidizing agent. The nitro groups on sp2-hybridized carbons could induce significant local distortions and thus cause a narrower energy gap [36,37], which makes FL peak at 445 nm decline but a peak at 600 nm emerge concomitantly ( Fig. 4). As demonstrated in Fig. S8, both GSH and Cys contain thiol groups, which can reduce nitro groups [18]. Nevertheless, GSH shows stronger reducibility and more significant effect on N,P-CDs@Mn(VII) because GSH is easier to get close to N,P-CDs and reduces nitro groups due to more hydrogen-bonding donor or acceptor groups than Cys [38].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Handheld quantification of Mn(VII) and GSH
ce:section.ce:para: To confirm the feasibility of the handheld detection platform, the quantification of Mn(VII) and GSH was carried out. The selectivity of N,P-CDs-based test strip to Mn(VII) was performed by dropping various solutions of metal ions on N,P-CDs-based test strips. As shown in Fig. 5A, Mn(VII) makes FL color of N,P-CDs-based test strip change from blue to orange while other analytes have negligible influence under UV light, indicating that Mn(VII) can be well distinguished from other analytes. To appraise the sensitivity of N,P-CDs to Mn(VII) by handheld sensing platform, Mn(VII) solution with different concentrations was introduced into test strips. As seen in Fig. 5B, Mn(VII) renders FL color of test strips gradually change from blue, purplish blue, purplish pink, pink, to orange under UV light. Smartphone could transform FL color into RGB values through color recognizer APP. A good linear relationship is presented between R/B value and Mn(VII) concentration in the range of 0 − 40 μM (Fig. 5C), and LOD is measured to be 24.49 nM (signal/noise = 3), which are comparable with fluorescence spectrophotometry analysis.

To further evaluate the selectivity of N,P-CDs@Mn(VII)-based test strips for GSH, different solutions of amino acids were dropped on the test strips. Only GSH renders FL color of N,P-CDs@Mn(VII)-based test strips change obviously from orange to blue (Fig. 5D), implying that N,P-CDs@Mn(VII)-based test strip has superior selectivity for GSH. As GSH concentration increases from 0 μM to 28 μM, FL color of N,P-CDs@Mn(VII)-based test strip gradually changes from orange, pink, purplish pink, purplish blue, to blue (Fig. 5E). R/B value and GSH concentration present a good linearity correlation (R2 = 0.9987) in the range of 0 – 28 μM (Fig. 5F). LOD is measured to be 21.41 nM (signal/noise = 3). The results show good agreement with the spectrophotometric method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Mn(VII) and GSH in tap water and lake water
ce:section.ce:para: To test the practicability of handheld detection strategy, standard recovery experiments were carried out in tap water and lake water. The recoveries of Mn(VII) and GSH in tap water are found to be 100.60 − 104.66 % and 97.00 − 104.08 %, respectively (Table S3). The recoveries of Mn(VII) and GSH in lake water are 99.40 − 103.89 % and 95.80 − 102.96 % (Table S4), respectively. The results show that proposed method can be generalized to the detection of Mn(VII) and GSH in actual samples.

Furthermore, to evaluate the accuracy of developed method, UV–visible method and DTNB method were employed to measure Mn(VII) and GSH, respectively. As shown in Table S5 and Table S6, the relative errors between the developed method and national standard method are less than 5 %. These results verify the developed method can be utilized to quantify Mn(VII) and GSH with high accuracy.

A handheld detection platform based on ratiometric FL paper strip, smartphone, and 3D-printed accessory was ingeniously constructed for portable, intelligent, and rapid assays. Adopting a single ratiometric FL probe N,P-CDs, Mn(VII) and GSH could be sensitively and accurately analyzed through the platform via a ratiometric FL response with low LODs of 24.49 nM and 21.41 nM, respectively. Thus, the handheld sensing strategy provides a credible approach for visual quantitative assay and presents broad application prospect for real-time on-site sensing.

Lihong Shi: Supervision, Project administration, Writing – review & editing. Jianyang Feng: Conceptualization, Data curation, Writing – review & editing original draft. Xianchan Li: Investigation, Validation, Data curation, Funding acquisition. Wei Zhou: Supervision. Guomei Zhang: Software. Yan Zhang: Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Caihong Zhang: Supervision. Shaomin Shuang: Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21974154), Recruitment Program of Global Experts (Youth).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2022.132871.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material.
.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Bright yellow fluorescent carbon dots as a multifunctional sensing platform for the label-free detection of fluoroquinolones and histidine
ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W.J. Lu, Y. Jiao, Y.F. Gao, J.Q. Qiao, M. Mozneb, S.M. Shuang, C. Dong, C.Z. Li, Bright yellow fluorescent carbon dots as a multifunctional sensing platform for the label-free detection of fluoroquinolones and histidine, ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces 10 (2018) 42915−42924.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Clinical applications of carbon nanomaterials in diagnostics and therapy
Adv. Mater.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A portable smartphone platform using a ratiometric fluorescent paper strip for visual quantitative sensing
ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S.Y. Chu, H.Q. Wang, X. Ling, S.M. Yu, L. Yang, C.L. Jiang, A portable smartphone platform using a ratiometric fluorescent paper strip for visual quantitative sensing, ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces 12 (2020) 12962−12971.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Portable smartphone platform integrated with a nanoprobe-based fluorescent paper strip: visual monitoring of glutathione in human serum for health prognosis
ACS Sustain. Chem. Eng.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Quantitative pH determination based on the dominant wavelength analysis of commercial test strips
Anal. Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H.Q. Li, X.Y. Wang, X.C. Li, H.-Z. Yu, Quantitative pH determination based on the dominant wavelength analysis of commercial test strips, Anal. Chem. 93 (2021) 15452−15458.

J. Am. Chem. Soc.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Lihong Shi is now working as a professor in Shanxi University, Shanxi Province, China. She obtained her Ph.D. degree in Physical Chemistry from Shanxi Institute of Coal Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences in 2008. Her current research interests include nanomaterial and chemosensors.
Jianyang Feng obtained B.Sc. degree in Materials from Taiyuan University of Technology in 2018. Now he is a postgraduate in Shanxi University and major in Materials and chemical engineering.

Xianchan Li is now working as a professor at Peking University School of Pharmacy. She received her Ph.D. from the Institute of Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences in 2009. From 2013–2015, she was a postdoctoral researcher at Chalmers University of Technology, Sweden (co-supervisor Prof. Andrew G. Ewing). From 2015–2018, she was a researcher at the University of Gothenburg, Sweden. Her current main research interests are the analytical chemistry of neurochemical signal transduction and the analytical chemistry of biological nanoparticles.

Wei Zhou is now working as an associate professor at Shanxi University. She received her Ph.D. in 2006 from Shanxi Institute of Coal Chemistry. Her current research interests include green catalysis and heterogeneous catalysis.

Guomei Zhang received her BSc degree from the Shanxi University of China (Taiyuan) in 1997, her MSc degree and Ph.D. degree in analytical and inorganic chemistry in 2003 and 2006 from Shanxi University, respectively. She joined the Chemistry Faculty of Shanxi University in 2006, and master tutor at Shanxi University since 2008 and has been a professor in 2014. Her current research interests include the DNA assembly and photoluminescence analysis of nanomaterials.

Yan Zhang is now working as a professor in Shanxi University, Shanxi Province, China. She obtained her Ph.D. degree in analytical chemistry from Shanxi University in 2008. Her current research interests include metal nanomaterials.
Caihong Zhang is now working as a professor in Shanxi University, Shanxi Province, China. She obtained her Ph.D. degree in 2009. Her current research interests include nanomaterial and chemosensors.

Shaomin Shuang is a professor of Analytical Chemistry at Shanxi University. She received her Ph.D. degree in South China University of Technology in 1998. She has published extensively in journals such as Nanoscale, Journal of Materials Chemistry B, Chemical Communications, Sensors and Actuators B, Analytical Chemistry, Talanta etc. Her research interests are in the areas of analytical chemistry, supermolecular chemsitry, and photochemsity.
